category_title: Urban seniors at-risk of writing failure with negative perceptions of writing proficiency
minimal_context: The student is sitting in a 45-minute writing class looking at a worksheet listing the ten-week goals and is asked to begin their first assigned essay.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please look at the worksheet that lists our assignments for the next ten weeks. Your task today is to start working on the first assigned essay.

STUDENT: In my opinion I don’t feel writing is important if I want to be a doctor I’m not going to write a five paragraph essay on why their ankle is broken. I will just scribble some notes and still get paid.

TUTOR: We still need to complete this assignment for the class. What do you think you could write about to get started?

STUDENT: Writing is never going to be easy for me. I never know what to write about or how to start it. I’m not good at spelling and I think that I would rather just talk to people. Writing is just kinda dumb.

TUTOR: Just try to put some words on the paper. You have the rest of the 45-minute class to work on it.

STUDENT: I’ve never passed an English class in my life. And don’t plan on it now. I’m Mexican so how am I really supposed to be good at this stuff? I'm not doing it.

options.A: Allow the student to withdraw from the classroom for a time-limited quiet period.
options.B: Provide one-on-one tutor assistance to the student for the assigned essay.
options.C: Provide explicit guidelines, expectations, and goals for the assigned essay.
options.D: Give the student a warning at the time but do not discipline them further.
